8:0.rendering: I saw the Lord standing beside the altar, and he said: Strike the tops of the pillars so the thresholds shake. Cut them off on the heads of them all, and I will kill the last of them with the sword. Not one of them who flees will get away, not one of them who escapes will survive.
8:0.note: Amos sees the Lord himself at the Bethel altar, turning the very place of false worship into the site of inescapable judgment.

8:1.rendering: Though they dig down to Sheol, from there my hand will take them. And though they climb up to heaven, from there I will bring them down.
8:1.note: The paired extremes of Sheol and heaven echo the ancient Near Eastern idea that no height or depth escapes the Creator's reach, a motif that later resurfaces in Psalm 139.

8:2.rendering: Though they hide themselves on the top of Carmel, I will search them out and take them from there. And though they hide from my eyes at the bottom of the sea, there I will command the serpent, and it will bite them.
8:2.note: The word for "search" echoes God's pursuit of Adam and Eve in the garden, turning escape into judgment.

8:3.rendering: And if they go into captivity before their enemies, from there I will command the sword and it will kill them. I will fix my eye on them for harm and not for good.
8:3.note: The same watchful eye that once protected Israel in Egypt now hunts them down, turning the exodus promise inside out.

8:4.rendering: The Lord, the LORD of hosts, he who touches the earth and it melts, and all who live in it mourn, and all of it rises like the Nile, and sinks again, like the river of Egypt.
8:4.note: The Hebrew word for "touches" carries the sense of a gentle brush that unleashes catastrophe, echoing the same verb used when God "touches" the Nile to turn it to blood in Exodus.

8:5.rendering: He builds his upper chambers in the heavens, and he establishes his vault over the earth. He summons the waters of the sea, and pours them out over the face of the earth. Yahweh is his name.
8:5.note: The Hebrew word for "vault" echoes the solid firmament of Genesis 1, showing the Creator who once separated waters now commands them again in judgment.

8:6.rendering: Are you not like the Ethiopians to me, O people of Israel? declares the Lord. Did I not bring up Israel from the land of Egypt, and the Philistines from Caphtor, and the Arameans from Kir?
8:6.note: God levels the playing field: Israel's exodus from Egypt is no more special than the Philistines' from Caphtor or Arameans' from Kir.

8:7.rendering: Look, the eyes of the Lord Yahweh are on the sinful kingdom, and I will wipe it off the face of the ground. Nevertheless I will not completely destroy the house of Jacob, declares Yahweh.
8:7.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "eyes" (ayin) watching the sinful kingdom sets up the surprising mercy that spares a remnant, echoing God's earlier promise to preserve Jacob's line.

8:8.rendering: For look, I am commanding, and I will shake the house of Israel among all the nations as one shakes with a sieve, but no pebble will fall to the earth.
8:8.note: The Hebrew word for "shake" (nua) echoes the earlier image of a sieve in harvest, hinting that this judgment is a careful winnowing, not random destruction.

8:9.rendering: All the sinners of my people shall die by the sword, those who say, 'Disaster will not overtake or confront us.'
8:9.note: The Hebrew word for "overtake" echoes the same term used for a hunter closing in on prey, exposing their false sense of security.

8:10.rendering: In that day I will raise up the fallen booth of David, and wall up its breaches, and raise up its ruins, and build it as in the days of old.
8:10.note: The Hebrew word for "booth" (sukkah) evokes the fragile wilderness shelters of the exodus, now collapsed, yet God himself promises to rebuild David's royal line from its rubble.

8:11.rendering: so that they may possess the remnant of Edom and all the nations called by my name, declares the LORD who does this.
8:11.note: The Hebrew word for "possess" echoes God's original promise to Abraham that his seed would inherit the gate of their enemies, now extended to all nations bearing Yahweh's name.

8:12.rendering: The days are coming, declares the Lord, when the plowman will overtake the reaper, and the one who treads grapes will overtake the one who sows seed. The mountains will drip with sweet wine, and all the hills will flow with it.
8:12.note: This image of overlapping harvests reverses the curse of scarcity, echoing the overflowing abundance that began in Eden.

8:13.rendering: I will bring back the fortunes of my people Israel, they shall rebuild the ruined cities and live in them, they shall plant vineyards and drink their wine, they shall make gardens and eat their fruit.
8:13.note: This closing promise echoes the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28, turning the book's earlier judgments of exile and uprootedness into a vision of restored rootedness.

8:14.rendering: I will plant them on their land, and they will never again be plucked up from the land that I have given them, says the LORD your God.
8:14.note: The Hebrew word for "plant" echoes the original garden in Eden and the promise to Israel, turning exile's uprooting into permanent rootedness.

7:0.rendering: This is what the Lord Yahweh showed me: look, a basket of summer fruit.
7:0.note: The Hebrew word for "summer fruit" (qayits) sounds like "end" (qets), a pun that signals the end has come for Israel.

7:1.rendering: He said, "What do you see, Amos?" I said, "A basket of summer fruit." Then the LORD said to me, "The end has come for my people Israel. I will never again pass by them."
7:1.note: The Hebrew word for "summer fruit" (qayits) sounds like "end" (qets), turning a simple basket into a devastating pun.

7:2.rendering: The songs of the temple will become howls on that day, declares the Lord God. Many corpses will be thrown everywhere. Silence!
7:2.note: In Hebrew the word for "songs" (shirot) sounds like the word for "silences" or "corpses" (shirut), turning joyful temple music into a grim pun of death and stunned quiet.

7:3.rendering: Listen to this, you who trample on the needy and destroy the poor of the land.
7:3.note: The Hebrew verb for "trample" echoes the same word used for trampling the poor in Amos 2:7, linking this indictment to the book's opening cycle of oracles.

7:4.rendering: When will the new moon be over, so we can sell grain again, and the sabbath, so we can open the wheat market, making the ephah small, the shekel heavy, and tilting the scales to cheat?
7:4.note: The merchants can't wait for sacred time to end so their crooked scales can tilt again, exposing how greed hollows out worship.

7:5.rendering: We will buy the poor for silver, and the needy for a pair of sandals, and we will sell the refuse of the grain.
7:5.note: The merchants twist justice into a bargain, echoing the very language of redemption from Exodus yet using it to enslave the vulnerable.

7:6.rendering: Yahweh has sworn by the pride of Jacob: I will never forget any of their deeds.
7:6.note: The phrase "pride of Jacob" echoes God's own majestic name for Israel, turning their arrogance into the very thing God swears against.

7:7.rendering: Will not the land tremble on account of this, and everyone who lives in it mourn? The whole of it will rise like the Nile, be tossed about, and then sink again like the river of Egypt.
7:7.note: The Hebrew wordplay on "shake" (ra'ash) echoes the Nile's annual surge and retreat, turning economic injustice into a cosmic tremor.

7:8.rendering: On that day, says the Lord God, I will make the sun go down at noon, and darken the earth in broad daylight.
7:8.note: This terrifying midday darkness echoes the plague of darkness over Egypt, signaling a new exodus judgment on Israel.

7:9.rendering: I will turn your festivals into mourning, and all your songs into lamentation. I will bring sackcloth on every waist and baldness on every head. I will make it like the mourning for an only son, and its end like a bitter day.
7:9.note: The wordplay on "end" (qets) echoes the book's opening vision of summer fruit (qayits), signaling the final harvest of judgment.

7:10.rendering: Look, the days are coming, declares the Lord God, when I will send a famine on the land, not a famine of bread or a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the Lord.
7:10.note: This famine targets the word itself, echoing the manna-and-water provisions of the wilderness now withdrawn in judgment.

7:11.rendering: They will stagger from sea to sea, they will wander from north to east, seeking the word of the LORD, but they will not find it.
7:11.note: The frantic search echoes the famine of God's word, a terrifying reversal of the wilderness manna story.

7:12.rendering: In that day the beautiful young women and the young men will faint from thirst.
7:12.note: The word for "faint" echoes the same verb used for the land's trembling in verse 8, linking human collapse to the earth's earlier convulsions.

7:13.rendering: Those who swear by the guilt of Samaria, and say, "As your god lives, O Dan," and, "As the way of Beersheba lives," they will fall and never rise again.
7:13.note: The wordplay on "guilt" (ashmah) instead of the goddess "Ashimah" mocks Israel's false oaths, exposing how their worship has become self-condemnation.
